attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the birth suite at 38 weeks gestation. She reports a two-hour history of vaginal fluid loss but no current uterine contractions. She has had one prior caesarean section. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 120/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. On abdominal palpation, the fetus is felt to be in a transverse lie. A sterile speculum examination confirms rupture of membranes with clear liquor. Cardiotocography (CTG) shows a baseline fetal heart rate of 140 beats per minute with normal variability and no decelerations.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is at highest risk of occurring in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
In this scenario, the patient presents with ruptured membranes and a fetus in transverse lie at term. The complication at highest risk of occurring is cord prolapse.

Cord prolapse is a serious obstetric emergency where the umbilical cord descends through the cervix ahead of the presenting part of the fetus. It is particularly likely to occur in situations where the presenting part does not fill the lower uterine segment, as in this case of transverse lie. When the membranes rupture, there is a significant risk that the cord will be swept down with the rush of amniotic fluid.

Placental abruption, while a serious complication, is not specifically associated with transverse lie or ruptured membranes. It is more commonly associated with trauma, hypertensive disorders, or substance abuse.

Uterine rupture is a rare but serious complication, more commonly associated with previous caesarean section, excessive use of oxytocin, or obstructed labour. While this patient has had a previous caesarean section and transverse lie can lead to obstructed labour if allowed to progress, uterine rupture is not the most immediate risk in this scenario.

Shoulder dystocia occurs during vaginal delivery when the anterior shoulder of the fetus becomes impacted against the maternal pubic bone. It is not relevant in this case of transverse lie, where vaginal delivery is not possible.

Postpartum haemorrhage is a potential complication of any delivery but is not specifically increased by the conditions described in this scenario.

Given the high risk of cord prolapse in this situation, immediate preparations should be made for emergency caesarean section. Continuous fetal heart rate monitoring is crucial to detect any signs of fetal distress that might indicate cord compression. The patient should be counselled about the risk of cord prolapse and advised to report immediately any sensation of something in the vagina or any gush of fluid.

This case highlights the importance of recognising high-risk situations for cord prolapse, such as malpresentation (including transverse lie) with ruptured membranes. Prompt recognition and preparedness can significantly improve outcomes if cord prolapse does occur.
learningPoints: In transverse lie with ruptured membranes, there is a high risk of cord prolapse.
Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate delivery to prevent fetal hypoxia and death.
Continuous fetal heart rate monitoring and preparation for immediate caesarean section are crucial in high-risk situations for cord prolapse.
references: Boushra M, Stone A, Rathbun KM. Umbilical cord prolapse. [Updated 2023 May 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532996/
